Control Arm.reasoning: The 'Patients and Methods' section on page 1 states: 'In this phase III study, 790 patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer were equally randomly assigned to receive either ADT in combination with docetaxel 75 mg/m² for up to six cycles or ADT alone.' The arm receiving 'ADT alone' serves as the comparison group without the experimental docetaxel treatment. This is further supported by Table 1 and Figures 1, 2, and 3, which consistently refer to 'ADT Alone' as one of the treatment arms.

Number of Arms Included.reasoning: The 'Patients and Methods' section on page 1 states, '790 patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer were equally randomly assigned to receive either ADT in combination with docetaxel 75 mg/m² for up to six cycles or ADT alone.' This indicates two distinct treatment arms. Figure 1 and Table 1 on pages 2 and 3, respectively, further confirm these two arms: 'ADT plus docetaxel' and 'ADT alone'.

Class of Agent in Treatment Arm 1.reasoning: The 'Purpose' section on page 1 states: 'Docetaxel added to androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT) significantly increases the longevity of some patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer.' The 'Patients and Methods' section on page 1 further clarifies: '790 patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer were equally randomly assigned to receive either ADT in combination with docetaxel 75 mg/m² for up to six cycles or ADT alone.' The 'Results' section on page 1 refers to the combination arm as 'chemohormonal therapy arm'. Thus, Treatment Arm 1 consists of docetaxel (a cytotoxic agent) and ADT (a hormonal agent), which is referred to as chemohormonal therapy.

Treatment Arm 1 Regimen.reasoning: The 'Patients and Methods' section on page 1 states: 'In this phase III study, 790 patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer were equally randomly assigned to receive either ADT in combination with docetaxel 75 mg/m² for up to six cycles or ADT alone.' The 'Procedures' section on page 3 further details: 'Eligible patients were randomly assigned at a one-to-one ratio to ADT alone versus ADT plus docetaxel at a dose of 75 mg/m² every 3 weeks for up to six cycles without daily prednisone.' Table 1 and Figure 3 (C and D) also present data for 'High volume/de novo' and 'Low volume/de novo' subgroups, where 'de novo metastatic disease' refers to synchronous metastases.

Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | M.reasoning: The 'DISCUSSION' section on page 5 states, 'In the smaller subgroup of patients with high-volume disease with PLT (n = 91), those who received ADT alone had a better outcome compared with their counterparts with de novo metastatic disease.' It also mentions, 'In contrast, for the low-volume subgroups, neither patients with de novo metastatic disease nor patients with PLT were found to derive a survival benefit from docetaxel (de novo metastatic low-volume disease: median OS, 58.3 months for the combination arm v 59.8 months for the ADT alone arm; HR for death in the combination arm, 0.86; 95% CI, 0.52 to 1.42; P = .55; low-volume metastatic disease with PLT: median OS, 69.6 months for the combination arm v not reached for the ADT alone arm; HR for death in the combination arm, 1.25; 95% CI, 0.60 to 2.60; P = .55; Table 1; Fig 3).'
